candidate_problems.text: What principled criterion distinguishes a genuinely shared generative structure from a merely convenient structural redescription? The conjecture depends on analogies being load-bearing, but it leaves open how to tell when a proposed abstract constraint-system is objectively explanatory rather than an underconstrained mapping that can be retrofitted to many domains. This is central because without such a criterion, the claim that analogy creates new knowledge risks collapsing into permissive reinterpretation.

How can novelty generated by structural analogy be nontrivially identified and measured? The conjecture says new knowledge arises from consequences not previously noticed in either domain, but it remains unresolved what counts as genuinely new rather than implicitly already contained in background knowledge, modeler ingenuity, or generic deductive closure. A sharper account is needed of where the epistemic gain occurs: in the analogy itself, in subsequent derivation, in empirical testing, or in criticism of failed transfer.

What are the boundary conditions under which differences between domains preserve a shared structure versus destroy it? The conjecture treats differing boundary conditions as sources of discovery, but it does not specify when transferred structure should survive deformation and when domain-specific mechanisms invalidate the analogy. This is an important open problem because progress depends on knowing which deviations are informative refinements of the shared structure and which are decisive refutations.
